nativeRange.habitat: Flowing waters from small creeks and streams to shallows of larger fast-flowing rivers, generally preferring rocky areas with cover; also reported from ponds and lakes. Rarely encountered in open water.

decorPreferences: rocks and rockwork to form caves
clay pots or coconut shells as spawning sites
sand or fine-gravel substrate (they dig extensively)
minimal or hardy rooted plants only; loose plants will be uprooted

diet.notes: An unfussy omnivore that accepts most prepared foods. A varied diet of quality pellets or flakes, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia, as well as occasional vegetable matter like blanched spinach or spirulina, tends to produce the best colour and breeding condition.

goodWith: Larger robust Central American cichlids in very large tanks (e.g. Jack Dempsey, Firemouth) — only with caution
Large armoured catfish such as Pterygoplichthys or Pictus catfish
Silver Dollars (in tanks large enough to give them refuge)
avoidWith: Any small or peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, livebearers) — will be harassed or killed
Long-finned or slow-moving fish (angelfish, gouramis, fancy goldfish) — fin nipping and harassment
Shrimp and snails — generally eaten or crushed
Other conspecifics outside a bonded pair — severe intraspecific aggression
Bottom-dwellers like Corydoras or kuhli loaches that share the substrate with a breeding pair

imageCandidates.notes: S. Olkowicz (User Hippocampus); 1800x1144. Tack-sharp full lateral profile of a female showing the diagnostic bold black vertical bars on a blue-grey body plus orange belly flush; textbook convict look.
Deanpemberton; 3456x2304. Sharp shot of two convicts among rockwork, both showing the crisp black barring; illustrates how this pair-forming cichlid is kept.
Timmy Toucan; 2406x1926. Sharp full lateral of the leucistic 'Pink Convict' morph (an alsoKnownAs trade name), showing the barless pinkish-white variant of the same species.

summary: The Convict Cichlid is a small, boldly striped Central American cichlid widely regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-accessible cichlids available, though it tends to be highly territorial and turns intensely aggressive when breeding.
careNotes: The Convict Cichlid is one of the most resilient and beginner-accessible cichlids, tolerating a wide range of water parameters — generally around 22–28 °C, pH 6.5–8.0, and moderately hard water. Despite a modest adult size that commonly falls between 8 and 13 cm, with some males reaching up to 15 cm, it is widely regarded as one of the most aggressive freshwater fish for its size. Outside of breeding, a bonded pair tends to be territorial around their chosen cave; once spawning begins, they will attack and frequently kill any tankmate that approaches their fry, regardless of that tankmate's size. For this reason the Convict Cichlid is best kept as a single pair in a species tank, or only alongside similarly robust Central American cichlids in tanks substantially larger than the minimum — a 90 cm, 150-litre aquarium is a workable floor for a pair alone, but 200 litres or more is strongly advised if any tankmates are kept. They are avid diggers and will uproot plants, rearrange the substrate, and excavate pits around spawning caves. Sand or fine gravel works well as a substrate, and plants that attach to rocks or driftwood — such as Anubias, Java fern, or Bolbitis — are the practical choices, as rooted plants will generally be dug up. Several colour morphs are commonly traded, including the leucistic 'Pink Convict' (also sold as the White Convict) and various marbled forms; all are the same species. The sexes are straightforward to tell apart: females develop orange to pink belly scales and stay smaller, while males grow larger, develop longer pointed fins, and often develop a small nuchal hump (a fatty lump on the forehead) with age.
breedingNotes: Among the easiest freshwater fish to breed and a classic 'first cichlid spawning' species. A compatible pair will form readily; provide a cave (clay pot, coconut shell, or rock crevice) which they will adopt and defend. The female lays up to ~300 eggs on the cave wall or a flat stone; both parents guard aggressively, with the female tending eggs and the male patrolling the territory. Eggs hatch in roughly 3–4 days and fry are free-swimming after a further 4–7 days. Parental care is excellent and parents may continue to herd fry for several weeks; pairs can spawn again every 2–3 weeks if fry are removed. Because they breed so readily and aggressively, prospective keepers should have a plan for offspring before pairing — local fish stores are frequently saturated and unwanted convict fry are a well-known surplus problem.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as effective primary because Aquarium Co-Op (the assigned slice primary) has no dedicated care-guide page for this species — only forum threads, which returned HTTP 403. Per the playbook's primary-fallback rule, the first secondary (SeriouslyFish) was promoted to primary role.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), native range, max size (10 cm SL), wild water parameters (pH 7.0–8.0, dH 9–20, T 20–36 C wild envelope), and biotope (flowing waters of Central America).

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Cross-checked hobbyist-side data: adult size (10–12.7 cm), tank size (30 gal minimum), temperature (26–29 C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (10–15 dH), lifespan (8–10 yr), aggressive temperament, mid-tank swim zone, and digging behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: A cave-spawning substrate breeder that pairs readily and spawns without intervention once given a suitable cave and slightly warmer water, ideally around 26–28 °C. Each clutch contains up to approximately 300 eggs, which typically hatch within 3–4 days; the fry become free-swimming after a further 4–7 days. Both parents participate in care throughout, and pairs can spawn again every 2–3 weeks if the fry are removed.
